coredata.dc:title: Investigation of photoluminescence mechanism of graphene quantum dots and evaluation of their assembly into polymer dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Shoujun
Wang, Lei
Li, Bo
Song, Yubin
Zhao, Xiaohuan
Zhang, Guoyan
Zhang, Shitong
Lu, Siyu
Zhang, Junhu
Wang, Haiyu
Sun, Hongbo
Yang, Bai
coredata.dc:description: The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are attractive nanomaterials with excellent optical and optoelectronic properties. However, the clear photoluminescence (PL) was difficult to be investigated due to the complicated structures of the reported GQDs. In this work, four kinds of GQDs were synthesized by organic methods with certain chemical structures. The PL mechanism of the GQDs was investigated by ultrafast spectroscopy. In these organic synthesized GQDs, intrinsic state depends on size, while the energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state decides their optical properties. As a result, the green fluorescence of the C42H18, C96H30 not only depends on the size, but also results from bright edge state. For large GQDs, the energy level of intrinsic state is lower than the edge state, which lead to the weak PL. Furthermore, the PL polymer dots (PDs) were prepared by assembling GQDs and polymeric surfactant. The PDs possessed perfect solubility in water and kept the PL behavior of the organic synthesized GQDs. The present methods and results will lead a new direction for investigating the PL mechanism of the GQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The optimized electron delocalization MO diagram of C42H18 and its band positions. (b) The optimized electron delocalization MO diagram of C96H30 and its band positions. (c) The UV–vis spectrum and PL spectra of C42H18. (d) The UV–vis spectrum and PL spectra of C96H30. The insets of c and d are photographs of C42H18 and C96H30 under the bright and UV light, respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The lifetime of the C42H18 (a and b) and C96H30 (c and d) at 375nm excitation and probed at different wavelength sections. (e and f) Similar femtosecond time-resolved PL dynamics of C42H18 and C96H30 (under 400nm excitation and probed at 520nm). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Transient spectral evolution of C42H18. (a) Transient absorption spectra with picosecond unit at 400nm excitation. (b) The decay tendency at different wavelength sections. (c) Transient absorption spectra with picosecond unit at 460nm excitation. (d) The decay tendency at different wavelength sections. Detailed transient spectral comparison and analysis. (e) Comparison between transient absorption spectra of short time scale. (f) Comparison between transient absorption spectra of long time scale. For panel e and f, the excited-state absorption signals are normalized, and vertical dash line is corresponding to the pump wavelength at 460nm. (g) Normalized TA dynamics for the characteristic wavelength corresponding to ground-state bleaching (430nm and 450nm) and stimulated emission (490nm) at 460nm excitation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Transient spectral evolution of C96H30. (a) Transient absorption spectra with picosecond unit at 400nm excitation. (b) The decay tendency at different wavelength sections. (c) Transient absorption spectra with picosecond unit at 460nm excitation. (d) The decay tendency at different wavelength sections. (e) The TA and PL decay spectra of the C96H30. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The suggested energy levels of the C42H18, C96H30, C132, C132H34 and C222H42. The size-dependent energy levels in these H-passivated small GQDs are in agreement with the molecule orbital calculation. The intrinsic state depends on size. Energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state decides its optical properties. The C132 was determined by the reported paper [19]. (b) The structures of C42H18, C96H30, C132H34 and C222H42. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The schematic illustration of PDs fabrication. (b) The UV–vis and PL spectra of C42H18 PDs. (c) The UV–vis and PL spectra of C96H30 PDs. The insets of b-c are photographs of C42H18 and C96H30 PDs under the bright and UV light, respectively. (d and e) The lifetime of the C42H18 and C96H30 as well as their PDs at 375nm excitation and probed at 515nm. The inserted tables are the statistics for the lifetime. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Investigation of photoluminescence mechanism of graphene quantum dots and evaluation of their assembly into polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science & Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are attractive nanomaterials with excellent optical and optoelectronic properties. However, the clear photoluminescence (PL) was difficult to be investigated due to the complicated structures of the reported GQDs. In this work, four kinds of GQDs were synthesized by organic methods with certain chemical structures. The PL mechanism of the GQDs was investigated by ultrafast spectroscopy. In these organic synthesized GQDs, intrinsic state depends on size, while the energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state decides their optical properties. As a result, the green fluorescence of the C42H18, C96H30 not only depends on the size, but also results from bright edge state. For large GQDs, the energy level of intrinsic state is lower than the edge state, which lead to the weak PL. Furthermore, the PL polymer dots (PDs) were prepared by assembling GQDs and polymeric surfactant. The PDs possessed perfect solubility in water and kept the PL behavior of the organic synthesized GQDs. The present methods and results will lead a new direction for investigating the PL mechanism of the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Original graphene is a kind of zero-band semiconductor, which is difficult to serve as a fluorescent material. To open up the bandgap and facilitate its applications, researchers usually choose to change the surface chemical groups of original graphene or convert it to 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [1–4]. GQDs, labeled as a new sort of fluorescent carbon-based material [5–8], have attracted increasing attention for their good biocompatibility, photoelectric, optical properties as well as convenient surface modification. For the reasons mentioned above, GQDs promise to be used in numerous areas: biomedical applications, optoelectronic devices, sensors and assembly composites, etc.

Graphene quantum dots attracted more attention compared with other kinds of carbon materials, because it is single layered with precise chemical structure and easy chemical modification [12–20]. As a result, GQDs are ideal model system to investigate the PL mechanism of the carbon-based materials [21–32]. Due to the different chemical structures of the reported GQDs, although the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of GQDs can be explained from the aspect of size, surface chemical groups and doping atoms, there is no universal agreement on the specific PL mechanism: triple carbene at the zigzag edges, charge transfer/resonance between amino and graphene core, intrinsic state/defect state, edge state, molecule state at the surface of GQDs, high-crystalline carbon core state, additional energy levels by doping and so on [9–11,21–23,25,26].

The GQDs were always prepared by the chemical synthetic strategies, which mainly contain cutting different carbon resources and carbonization from small molecules. Besides, the organic synthesis is another efficient route to prepare GQDs [12–20]. Through organic synthesis, the structure of GQDs can be precisely controlled, leading to comprehending the PL mechesium of GQDs unambiguously. It is not surprising that steady-state spectroscopy has been considered and utilized as efficient mean to reveal the PL origin of the GQDs [33]. However, it cannot obtain enough information, because some crucial details are hidden in the steady-state characterizations. It also implies that these relevant events may happen very fast which are even far beyond instrument resolution of nanosecond time-resolved equipment. Therefore, a requirement for detailed ultrafast dynamics study on GQDs by femtosecond time-resolved spectroscopy becomes indispensable, which could make important contributions to the PL mechanism of GQDs [34,35].

In this paper, four kinds of GQDs (C42H18, C96H30, C132H34 and C222H42) were synthesized through organic routes [13–15,35,36]. The PL mechanism of the GQDs was investigated by ultrafast spectroscopy. Intrinsic state depends on size, while the energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state decides their optical properties in these organic synthesized GQDs. As a result, the green fluorescence of the C42H18, C96H30 is not only dependent on the size, but also on the bright edge state. For large GQDs (C132H34 and C222H42), the intrinsic state is lower than the edge state, which lead to the weak photoluminescence. Furthermore, PL polymer dots (PDs) were prepared by assembling GQDs and polymeric surfactant (tween 60) using a modified nanoprecipitation method. The PDs possessed perfect solubility in water and kept the PL behavior of the organic synthesized GQDs.

Diphenylacetylene, tetraphenylcyclopentadienone and 1,3,5-Triethynylbenzene were purchased from Aldrich. Iron (III) chloride was purchased from Tianjin Huadong Reagents. All the other reagents were purchase from Aldrich or Beijing Chemical Works.

Diphenylacetylene (1g) and tetraphenylcyclopentadienone (2.6g) were dissolved in o-xylene (40mL) under an argon atmosphere and the resultant mixture was heated for 18h at 170°C. After cooling, the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation. The products were purified by column chromatography on silica gel (petrol ether/dichloromethane). The obtained hexaphenylbenzene was ca. 3.3g. Then, hexaphenylbenzene (0.7g) was dissolved in dry dichloromethane (200mL) and argon continuously bubbled through the solution. Then dry iron (III) chloride (4.6g), dissolved in nitromethane (30mL), was dropwise added by a funnel. The reaction was kept for 12h and replenished the dichloromethane to keep the volume dose. Then, the reaction was quenched by adding methanol (350mL). The precipitate was collected by filtration, washed successively with methanol and dichloromethane and dried to afford a dark brown solid. The products were purified by column chromatography on silica gel (developing solvent: THF/water, v/v=7:3). Then, the dried products were purified by column chromatography on silica gel (developing solvent: toluene) again.

Triethynylbenzene (0.66g) and tetraphenylcyclopentadienone (1.534g) were dissolved in o-xylene (20mL) under an argon atmosphere and the resultant mixture was heated for 18h at 170°C. After cooling, the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation. The products were purified by column chromatography on silica gel. Then, the DPP (0.35g) was dissolved in dry dichloromethane (150mL) and argon continuously bubbled through the solution. Then dry iron (III) chloride (2.29g), dissolved in nitromethane (15mL), was dropwise added by a funnel. The reaction was kept for 18h and replenished the dichloromethane to keep the volume dose. Then, the reaction was quenched by adding methanol (300mL). The precipitate was collected by filtration and dried to afford a yellow–brown solid. The products were purified by column chromatography on silica gel (developing solvent: THF/water, v/v=7:3). Then, the dried products were purified by column chromatography on silica gel (developing solvent: toluene) again.
Preparation of C132H34 and C222H42 was obtain as the reported methods [35,36].

A THF solution (1mL) containing C42H18 or C96H30 (1.5mg) and tween 60 (4mg) was poured into water (9mL). This was followed by sonicating the mixture for 120s. After evaporation of THF by stirring the suspension at room temperature for 12h, the polymer dot suspension was purified through a 0.2μm syringe driven filter. After that, the PDs suspension was purified by dialysis (molecular weight cutoff 8000–14,000Da).

High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HTEM) was recorded on FEI Tecnai F20. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. IR spectra were taken on a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The fluorescent images were taken at Olympus BX51 at UV excitation. Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization reflect time-of-flight (MALDI-TOF) technique was recorded on Bruker autoflex speed TOF with tetracyanoquinodimethane (TCNQ) as the matrix. NMR was done on AVANCEIII500 (Bruker).

Nanosecond fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed by the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system under right-angle sample geometry. A 379/405nm picosecond diode laser (Edinburgh Instruments EPL375, repetition rate 2MHz) was used to excite the samples. The fluorescence was collected by a photomultiplier tube (Hamamatsu H5783p) connected to a TCSPC board (Becker&Hickel SPC-130). Time constant of the instrument response function (IRF) is about 300ps. Subpicosecond time resolved emission were measured by the femtosecond fluorescence upconversion method. A mode-locked Ti:sapphire laser/amplifier system (Solstice, Spectra-Physics) was used. The output of the amplifier of 1.5-mJ pulse energy, 100fs pulse width, at 800nm wavelength is split into two equal parts; the second harmonic (400nm) of one beam was focused in the sample as excitation. The resulted fluorescence was collected and focused onto a 1mm thick BBO crystal with a cutting angle of 35°. The other part of the RGA output was sent into an optical delay line and served as the optical gate for the up-conversion of the fluorescence. The generated sum frequency light was then collimated and focused into the entrance slit of a 300mm monochromator. A UV-sensitive photomultiplier tube 1P28 (Hamamatsu) was used to detect the signal. The FWHM of instrument response function was about 400fs.

The TA setup consisted of pump pulses doubled from laser pulses (∼100fs duration, 250Hz repetition rate) generated from a mode-locked Ti: sapphire laser/amplifier system (Solstice, Spectra-Physics) and broadband white-light probe pulses generated from 2-mm-thick water. The TA data were collected by a fiber-coupled spectrometer connected to a computer. The group velocity dispersion of the transient spectra was compensated by a chirp program. All the measurements were performed at room temperature. Pump-power dependent measurements are carried out. In the acceptable range (100–400nJ/pulse), no pump intensity dependent dynamics are observed.

The C42H18, C96H30 were prepared by Diels–Alder reactions as shown in Fig. S1a and b. The molecules were confirmed by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization reflect time-of-flight (MALDI-TOF), 1H NMR and IR spectra (Figs. S1c and d, S2 and S3). Firstly, we performed theoretical calculations on these two GQDs to elucidate excited state behaviors. Fig. 1 a and b present the optimized electron delocalization MO diagram of these two GQDs. Complete delocalization of the electrons can be observed in both of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels. And these two GQDs possess similar electron distributions except at the edge sections. The calculated band gaps were 3.592eV (absorption peak 345nm) and 2.666eV (absorption peak 465nm) for C42H18 and C96H30, respectively. Further, the optical properties were characterized by UV–vis and fluorescent spectra. The absorbance peak of C96H30 at 455nm is assigned to π–π∗ transition [28,32]. A very interesting phenomenon is that both two GQDs possess bright green emission, and the emission wavelength keeps still as the change of excitation wavelength. It should be noted that the PL emission curve can be divided into three symmetrical distributed peaks, which may be corresponding to the different excitation states [38].

It should be noted that the C42H18 possessed wide absorption peak, which covered a range of 450–200nm. Besides the expected absorption peak at 350nm for C42H18, there was still a weak absorption band at ca. 455nm. The reason is that the C42H18 tend to aggregate, leading to undesired assembly [13,14]. Furthermore, the deconvolution of the PL profile of C42H18 and C96H30 was provided under the different excitation wavelength [39,40]. It was very interesting that PL profile of both C42H18 and C96H30 could be divided into three peaks. The positions of these peaks keep unchanged at different excitation wavelength (Figs. S4 and S5). Such single PL behavior should not result from the multiple process, and it indirectly proved that the similar PL origin of these kinds of GQDs [26]. The quantum yields were 8.0% for C42H18 and 3.6% for C96H30, respectively (Table S1). The PL property of such GQDs kept stable in a wide range of temperature (Fig. S6), which was very important for the practical applications.

To gain more insight into the origin of their PL for these two kinds of GQDs, the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) was performed, which is highly important and necessary for clarifying the PL mechanism [41]. From Fig. 2 and Table S2, these long PL lifetimes over the whole emission spectra of both C42H18 and C96H30 can be fitted by two exponential components. The lifetime of the C42H18 and C96H30 was obtained at 375nm excitation and probed at different wavelength sections. Taking the lifetime values at 520nm as an example, the average PL lifetime of C96H30 (4.3ns) was slightly larger than that of C42H18 (4.0ns). All of the PL lifetimes of the C96H30 and C42H18 were less than 10ns. It indicated both GQDs possessed only fluorescent property without any phosphorescence [19]. It was different from the previously reported larger GQDs, i.e., C132, which possessed both fluorescence and phosphorescence (The phosphorescence always possess long lifetime) [19].

In order to deeply understand PL properties of these two kinds of small GQDs, we further investigated these samples by femtosecond time-resolved PL dynamics [42]. Surprisingly, we found the sub-nanosecond PL decay traces of both GQDs were similar to each other under 400nm exciting and probed at the central emission wavelength of 520nm (Fig. 2e and f). There was only a 1–2ps fast component with small fraction (10–20%). It is followed by a nanosecond lifetime, which was consistent with TCSPC data. It indicated both C42H18 and C96H30 had similar emission centers, which dominated the radiative recombination processes. What is the relationship between these green emission centers and the quantum confined energy levels? To address this question, femtosecond transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy was performed. Usually, there are three kinds of spectral features in the TA spectrum. After the pump light excitation, due to the Pauli exclusion principle, the filling of quantum electronic states will lead to the bleaching of the corresponding optical transitions from ground state to excited state, which is named ground-state bleaching (GSB). On the other hand, the photoexcited electrons in the excited states could further absorb probe light to higher levels or return to ground state by stimulated radiation due to the disturbance of probe light, which is the so-called excited-state absorption (ESA) and stimulated emission (SE), respectively. Among the features, only excited-state absorption has positive signals, the other has negative signals. All of these features reflect the information about the change of photogenerated carrier populations in corresponding energy levels [34,35].

Lingam et al. pioneered the edge-state PL of GQDs: the passivation of edge leads to quenching PL of the GQDs [26]. Inspired by this work, we investigated the so-called edge state for the green-photoluminescence GQDs and carbon nanodots [44]. At present, we applied this method to clarify the PL origin of C42H18 and C96H30. We examined both cases in 400 and 460nm excitation (Fig. 3 ). For C42H18, pumped by high-energy 400nm pulse (Fig. 3a and b), it gave the negative signals in the range of 400–500nm. This was in agreement with its steady-state absorption spectrum in the same wavelength range. Hence, these negative signals were ground-state bleaching signals, which were related to the intrinsic state of C42H18. At around 500nm corresponding to PL, there was an obvious spectral overlapping between negative stimulated emission signal and positive excited-state absorption. For long wavelength over 600nm, these positive signals can be assigned to pure excited-state absorption, and these wavelength-dependent TA dynamics, which were all longer than PL decay dynamics reminded us that the true PL center was not contained in such 400nm excitation TA spectra. To scrutinize the origin of PL, we carried the TA experiment in 460nm excitation. This excitation wavelength is near to the peak in PLE spectra of C42H18 corresponding to the green PL (Fig. S7). So it can be expected that in this case the main emission centers will be fully excited.

From Fig. 3c and d, there was a cupped shape, which indicated the PL center was excited [44]. Compared with the short and long time scale in both excitation conditions respectively (Fig. 3e and f), we can see that when the state at around 450nm was excited, a remarkably enhanced negative signal at ca. 490nm corresponding to the region of PL appeared. The characteristic wavelength dynamics of 430, 450 and 490nm in 460nm excitation showed the same decay traces (Fig. 3g). Since both ground-state bleaching and stimulated emission were negative signals, and when photo-generated carriers returned to the ground state through a radiative recombination channel, both signals were synchronously recovered. Hence, these negative signals in 460nm excitation were attributed to the overlapping between the ground-state bleaching at 450nm and stimulated emission. The theoretical calculation and steady-state fluorescence of C42H18 (Fig. 1a) have demonstrated there was another PL center controlled the PL behavior instead of intrinsic state. From the TA analysis under 400 and 460nm excitation, and considering the chemical structure of the C42H18, the PL center was controlled by either the π-electron system or the edge form. We can conclude the true PL center was originated from this edge state [26,38].

For C96H30, both 400 and 460nm excitation TA experiments gave the same spectral shape in the whole probe window (Fig. 4 ). These negative ground-state bleaching signals were consistent with the steady-state absorption spectrum well [43]. In the range of 500–600nm, due to the overlapping between stimulated emission and excited-state absorption, there was a cupped shape for these positive signals. Interestingly, its ground-state bleaching signal recovered very fast within the first 50ps, followed by a long-lifetime process. In comparison with its PL decay dynamics (Fig. 4e), we can conclude this fast decay of ground state was corresponding to a non-radiative process. Because of the different dynamics of the TA and PL in C96H30, we can give the following interpretation: Although the TA behaviors were similar under the 400 and 460nm excitation for C96H30, there were different states for these two spectra; the intrinsic state was figured out in the TA of 400nm excitation while the edge state was appeared in the TA of 460nm excitation. It is likely due to a photo-generated carrier transfer from lightless carbon core to emission centers at the edge of these graphene sheets [32]. The photogenerated carriers are at first stored in the carbon backbone, then leak fast into any possible emission states (the whole graphene structure or edge structure) and even traps (meaningless structures).

The size-dependent energy levels in these H-terminated small GQDs were in agreement with the molecule orbital calculation. Larger size, smaller band gap and energy spacing are according to the energy band in steady-state absorption spectra which located at 284, 315 and 360nm for C42H18; 400, 425 and 455nm for C96H30. We further demonstrated that their green PL was not from the quantum confined intrinsic state, but came from bright edge state. Thus, the energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state (emission centers) decides their optical properties in these organic synthesized GQDs (Fig. S8). If the energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state was large enough, for example, in our cases, it could be estimated to be ca. 1eV for C42H18 and 0.34eV for C96H30, respectively, the fluorescence is dominant (Fig. 5 a). If the energy level offset was small enough (meeting thermal activation condition, ∼kBT), the long carrier lifetime in intrinsic state will give a possibility for intersystem crossing from singlet excited state to triple excited state of edge state, such as previously reported C132 [18,19,45]. The serial GQDs, C132H34 and C222H42, were also prepared (Fig. 5b) to further explain the PL mechanism [36,37]. However, the fluorescence was too weak to be investigated by existing equipment (the C222H42 was even hardly dissolved). In the cases of C132H34 and C222H42, the core state possibly decreased and was lower than the edge state, as a result, the C132H34 and C222H42 lost the expected fluorescence.

The prepared GQDs were kinds of hydrophobic materials, which limited their applications in aqueous or bio-based environments. To expand the applications of the organic GQDs, one of polymeric surfactant (tween 60) was used as the encapsulation matrix to yield GQDs-loaded PDs. Fig. 6 a shows the schematic illustration of the PDs formation by a modified nanoprecipitation method [46]. When a THF solution of GQDs and tween 60 was added to water under continuous sonication, PDs can be formed by self-assembly route. The hydrophobic part of tween 60 and the GQDs are intertwined as the core, and hydrophilic chains of tween 60 forms the outer shell. After THF removal, the PDs were further purified by passing samples through a 220nm membrane and dialysis. The typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the PDs is shown in Fig. S9. The images indicated that the PDs were well dispersed and have a spherical morphology with a mean size of ca. 8–14nm. Fig. 6b and c shows the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the PDs suspension in water, which kept the optical properties of the as-prepared GQDs. From the fluorescence lifetime of the PDs, the two exponential components and the average lifetimes changed little compared with the organic synthesized GQDs (Fig. 6d and e). The PDs also exhibit superior photobleaching resistance, which may be benefited by the polymer protection (Fig. S10) [47]. For possible bio-based applications, the PDs should be further evaluated or chemical modification [48–50].

In conclusion, a series of GQDs (C42H18, C96H30, C132H34, C222H42) were synthesized by organic methods. The PL mechanism of the GQDs was investigated by ultrafast spectroscopy [51]. We draw the conclusion that in these organic synthesized GQDs, intrinsic state depends on size, while the energy level offset between intrinsic state and edge state decides their optical properties. As a result, the green fluorescence of these GQDs not only depends on the size, but also results from bright edge state. This exciting result will provide a direction for the researchers to understand the PL mechanism of many carbon-based materials. Furthermore, PL PDs were prepared by assembling GQDs and polymeric surfactant. The PDs possessed perfect solubility in water and kept the PL behavior of the as-prepared GQDs. The present work exploited a new method to prepare the PDs, which also have function-integration capacity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51373065, 81320108011, 21221063, 21273096), the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, Grant No. 2012CB933800 and 2014CB921302), and the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (No. 20130061130010). The authors also acknowledges the "111" Program under Grant No. B06009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.05.051.
